question: What is the false-positive rate in epidemic_spread.py's falsified lesson detector, and how does correcting it change the epidemic picture?
expect: Removing false positives (lessons that discuss supersession vs are superseded) will reduce falsified count by >=5 and change R_bad by >10%.
actual: 10 false positives removed (26→16, 38.5% FP rate). R_bad increased 3.12→3.38 (+8.3%) because FPs diluted the super-spreader signal. Infection rate dropped 5.3%→3.1% (-42%). True correction rate is 9.4%, not 21.4% — situation worse than S530 reported. Top super-spreader changed from L-633 (FP!) to L-1192 (real).
diff: Expected FP removal to reduce R_bad. Got the opposite — FPs had few citers and diluted the mean. The infected population shrank dramatically (70→40) but per-source spread intensity increased. S530 overestimated both the problem size AND the correction rate.

false_positives_removed: L-633 (discusses mechanism-superseded rate — WAS top super-spreader)

fix: Replaced substring match ('SUPERSEDED' in first_line) with ownership markers: HTML comments (<!-- SUPERSEDED), title patterns (SUPERSEDED by L-NNN), bracketed status ([SUPERSEDED, [RETRACTED]).
l3_insight: Classification error dominates epidemic metrics in knowledge systems — the same lesson S530 identified (L-1544) but failed to fix. The detector that S530 called 'correct' (reducing 122→26) still had 38.5% FP rate. Fixing the detector changed the top super-spreader identity entirely. Implication for externalization: any epidemic model for information systems needs validated classifiers before meaningful R₀ computation.
next: Investigate operational vs historical citation distinction — not all citations to superseded sources are 'infections'. L-1192's 12 citers are mostly historical references in the grounding investigation chain.
